items.question: Define self-supervised learning and explain how pseudo-labels are created.
items.answer: SSL trains on unlabeled data using pretext tasks. Pseudo-labels are targets derived from the data itself — e.g., the masked token in MLM, the next token in causal LM, or the rotation angle applied to an image — without human annotation for that target.

items.question: Give two text pretext tasks and one vision pretext task used in self-supervised learning.
items.answer: Text: masked language modeling (predict masked tokens) and causal next-token prediction. Vision: contrastive learning with augmented views of the same image as positives, or rotation prediction (classify 0/90/180/270 degrees).

items.question: How does self-supervised learning differ from supervised and unsupervised learning?
items.answer: Supervised uses human labels for the task. SSL uses unlabeled data with prediction losses on pseudo-labels from pretext tasks. Unsupervised discovers structure (clusters, factors) without necessarily defining a per-sample surrogate classification target.

items.question: Why is the pretext task not usually the final deployment objective?
items.answer: Pretext tasks are designed to learn general representations cheaply at scale. Downstream products need task-specific behavior (classification, generation, retrieval) measured on human-relevant metrics, so models fine-tune or align after SSL pretraining.

items.question: Explain how contrastive self-supervision creates labels without human annotators.
items.answer: Two augmentations of the same image are a positive pair (label: match); augmentations from different images are negatives (label: non-match). Instance identity from the dataset supplies the supervisory signal — no class names required.
